pattern: Repeatedly cycling every letter of one word forward is the same as picking a single fixed shift amount, so two words are related exactly when subtracting each word's own first letter from all of its letters produces the same pattern.
hints: Applying the cyclic next-letter operation over and over to a whole string is equivalent to picking one shift amount between 0 and 25 and applying it once; a word can only ever become one of 26 fixed variants of itself.

Because the operation can be applied to either word in a pair, two words are related exactly when there is some shift amount that turns one into the other, and shifting a word by k is undone by shifting the other word by 26 minus k.

Normalize every word by subtracting its own first letter's value from every letter's value (wrapping around the alphabet); two words are related exactly when their normalized forms are identical, so group words by that normalized form and add up n*(n-1)/2 for each group of size n.

constraints.note: each words[i] length is 1..100000, lowercase English letters only, and the total number of characters across all words (n * m) is at most 100000

edge_inputs.description: the reference example with exactly one similar pair
the reference example with a wrap-around shift and a repeated word
a single word, no pair possible
three identical words form three similar pairs
single-letter words are always mutually similar since any single letter can be shifted into any other
words that each look like a simple increasing or decreasing run but land on distinct normalized patterns
an alphabet wrap on one word must not accidentally coincide with a non-wrapping shift of another
two long identical words near the n times m character budget

stress.description: a moderate number of longer words built from a handful of shifted base patterns, so several similar groups exist
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 50))
    m = 500
    base_count = max(1, n // 4)
    bases = [''.join(rng.choice('abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz') for _ in range(m)) for _ in range(base_count)]
    words = []
    for _ in range(n):
        base = rng.choice(bases)
        k = rng.randint(0, 25)
        shifted = ''.join(chr((ord(c) - 97 + k) % 26 + 97) for c in base)
        words.append(shifted)
    rng.shuffle(words)
    return [words]

stress.description: many short words, mostly random with a few deliberately similar pairs mixed in
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 5000))
    m = 5
    words = []
    for _ in range(n):
        words.append(''.join(rng.choice('abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz') for _ in range(m)))
    for _ in range(n // 10):
        i = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        k = rng.randint(0, 25)
        words[i] = ''.join(chr((ord(c) - 97 + k) % 26 + 97) for c in words[0])
    return [words]
